course_description:
This postgraduate-level course provides an introduction to research methods in applied linguistics, with a particular focus on language use, learning, and teaching. It familiarizes students with key concepts in academic research, including research design, approaches to data analysis, and ethical considerations. The course offers an overview of both qualitative and quantitative research methods, introducing students to their applications in areas such as language acquisition, language variation, and educational contexts. Students will engage with foundational readings and discussions on methodological approaches while developing a basic understanding of how research is conducted in applied linguistics.

A central focus of the course is building essential research skills, including academic writing, critical reading, and the core steps of planning and structuring a research project. Students will also gain experience in formulating research questions, evaluating sources, and presenting their ideas clearly, both in writing and orally. While this course serves as an introduction, it lays the groundwork for more advanced study and practical application in later courses.

This is a postgraduate-level course on academic research methods in language and culture studies, with a particular focus on the areas of language use, learning, and teaching. The course covers a wide variety of issues and topics raised in current and seminal works on language acquisition across ages, in different communities, in and out of the classroom context. It is designed to familiarise the students with a range of qualitative and quantitative research techniques and ethical research practices. The students are also introduced to the core steps of conceptualising, designing, undertaking, and presenting (orally and in writing) a research project, which are the essential skills for postgraduate students and academics.

In order to optimise the student experience, it may be necessary to reallocate students to a different class from their first choice. Before this happens, every effort will be made to enable students to voluntarily change into an alternative class that is suitable. Please note: Teaching staff do not have access to the timetabling system to help with class allocation.

Weekly learning activities consist of a one-hour face-to-face or online lecture and a two-hour tutorial.ﾠ Weekly lecture content consists of pre-recorded lectures that are uploaded to Blackboard one week before the scheduled week.ﾠ Weekly tutorials are conducted face-to-faceﾠ following your scheduled timetable allocations. Throughout the semester you will be expected to contribute substantially and consistently to our tutorial activities and discussions of research approaches and methodologies drawing on your own ideas and experiences. Please make sure that you keep up with the course content by reviewing the online materials made available on Blackboard (e.g., lecture podcasts, lecture and tutorial slides, readings) and completing the assessments.
course_aims: The course aims to introduce research methodology in language and culture studies (with a particular interest in language). It willﾠpresent the fundamental skills needed to plan and carry out various types of postgraduate research,ﾠranging from critical reading, to structuringﾠcourse papers andﾠdissertation, to basic practical data collection and analysis tools and techniques.ﾠTopics examined include the nature and types of research, research quality and planning, honesty and ethics in research, library and database searches, developing a researchﾠtopic and literature review, preparing a research proposal, andﾠcritical reading and writing skills. Course assessments will provide the opportunity to apply these skills in relevant research tasks.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand the nature and process of research and familiarise yourself with different research areas in language and culture studies
Comprehend the basic aspects and components of research methodology, from the inception of a research project to data collection, analysis, and presentation
Critically review, analyse, and synthesise findings from a range of secondary sources
Design and present your research proposal in a manner conforming to accepted academic standards in both written and spoken form
Identify, locate, and effectively use research materials relevant to your field of study

assessment_details.title: Research Methods Learning Journal
assessment_details.due_date: WEEK 4 - 16/03/2026 1:00 pm WEEK 5 - 23/03/2026 1:00 pm WEEK 6 - 30/03/2026 1:00 pm WEEK 7 - 13/04/2026 1:00 pm WEEK 8 - 20/04/2026 1:00 pm WEEK 9 - 27/04/2026 1:00 pm WEEK 10 - 5/05/2026 1:00 pm WEEK 11 - 11/05/2026 1:00 pm WEEK 12 - 18/05/2026 1:00 pm WEEK 13 - 25/05/2026 1:00 pm 1pm, Monday of designated weeks (students must complete at least 5 of the 10 available submissions).
assessment_details.task_description: The Research Methods Learning Journal is a reflective assessment designed to support your ongoing engagement with course content and to encourage the gradual development of your understanding of research design, methodology, and ethical practice in Applied Linguistics. Across the semester, you are required to submit five short reflective journal entries , each based on a selected session of the course. Each entry should be between 100 and 200 words in length and is worth 3 per cent of the final grade, contributing a total of 15 per cent . PURPOSE The purpose of this assessment is to promote regular reflection on your learning and to support you in connecting theoretical discussions of research methods to your own developing research interests. Through these reflections, you will consolidate your understanding of research design, identify areas of uncertainty, and consider how different methodological approaches may be applied to empirical research in Applied Linguistics. CONTENT Each journal entry should reflect on your learning from a particular session of the course and engage meaningfully with the content covered. You may consider what you learned about research methods or research design during that session, which methodological approaches or analytical techniques were introduced, what aspects you found challenging or unclear, and how the concepts discussed could inform the design of a research project in Applied Linguistics. Not all aspects need to be addressed in every entry; rather, entries should demonstrate thoughtful, reflective, and substantive engagement with the session content. STATEMENT ON AI & MT This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. Whilst students may use generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools and/or Machine Translation (MT) technologies to support their preparation, such use must be appropriately acknowledged or referenced in line with UQ’s academic integrity expectations . A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Over the course of the semester, students are required to submit five Research Methods Learning Journal entries .
Journal entries must be submitted as text responses via Blackboard. Entries must be submitted by Monday at 1pm of the week following the session on which the reflection is based. For example, the Journal entry for Week 3 must be submitted by Monday 1pm of Week 4. Journal entries may be submitted for sessions between Weeks 3 and 12.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
The reduced extension period is intended to ensure the timely progression and completion of assessment tasks.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: In-Class Presentation and Reflective Task
assessment_details.task_description: The In-Class Presentation requires you to work in groups of two to four students to deliver an in-class presentation based on an assigned chapter from Research Methods in Applied Linguistics (Phakiti & Paltridge, Eds.), accompanied by an individual reflective submission. The task is designed to deepen your engagement with research methods content, enhance your academic communication skills, and encourage critical reflection on methodological choices and issues in Applied Linguistics research. PURPOSE This assessment evaluates your ability to understand, synthesise, and communicate research methods knowledge, as well as to reflect critically on your own learning. While the presentation component focuses on collaborative engagement with course material, the reflective component ensures that all students demonstrate individual understanding and meaningful engagement with the methodological issues discussed. PRESENTATION Each group will deliver a 20 to 30-minute in-class presentation on an assigned chapter that complements, but does not duplicate, the tutorial reading for that week. Presentations will be scheduled between Weeks 4 and 12. The presentation should provide a clear and coherent overview of the chapter’s key concepts, research approaches, and methodological insights, and should include a short in-class activity designed to actively engage peers with the content. Presentations will be delivered in class and recorded for moderation purposes. REFLECTIVE COMMENTARY Following the presentation, students are required to submit a 400-word individual reflective commentary. This submission must include a reflective commentary on what was learned from the group presentation. The reflection should engage with the content of the presentation and the process of working together, highlight key insights or challenges, and consider how the content may inform the student’s own research interests or proposal. STATEMENT ON AI & MT This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. Whilst students may use generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools and/or Machine Translation (MT) technologies to support their preparation, such use must be appropriately acknowledged or referenced in line with UQ’s academic integrity expectations . A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The reflective commentary must be submitted via Blackboard at 4PM on the Friday of the week following the presentation.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Research Proposal
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment requires you to submit a written research proposal of 2,000 words (not counting references or appendices) outlining the design, rationale, and significance of an original research project in Applied Linguistics. The proposal represents the culmination of your learning in the course and provides an opportunity to demonstrate your ability to conceptualize, design, and critically evaluate a feasible research study. PURPOSE This assessment evaluates your ability to define a research problem, engage critically with relevant literature and theory, formulate a clear research question, design an appropriate and ethical methodology, and reflect on the feasibility, limitations, and contribution of your proposed study. STRUCTURE The proposal should begin with an introduction that outlines the research topic, establishes its relevance within Applied Linguistics, and provides an overview of the proposal structure. This should be followed by a literature review that synthesises relevant research, identifies key theoretical perspectives, and motivates the research focus. The proposal should then clearly state the research question or hypothesis and justify it in relation to the literature. The methodology section should describe the proposed research design, including context, participants where relevant, data collection methods, and data analysis procedures, and should demonstrate that the proposed approach is ethical, appropriate, and feasible. The proposal should conclude with a critical reflection on the significance, limitations, and anticipated challenges of the study. A complete reference list in APA 7 format must be included. STATEMENT ON AI & MT This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools and/or Machine Translation (MT) technologies to support their preparation, such use must be appropriately acknowledged or referenced in line with UQ’s academic integrity expectations . A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. Indeed, to pass this assessment, students are required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission during a follow-up one-on-one interview independent of AI and MT tools. The interview will be conducted live (in person or via Zoom), and the use of generative AI or MT tools will therefore not be permitted during the interview itself. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The Research Proposal must be submitted via Turnitin as a single Word or PDF file. The proposal must not exceed 2,000 words, excluding references, and must adhere to APA 7 formatting and referencing conventions.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
While a maximum extension of 28 calendar days may be approved for the written submission, please note that an extension for the written component will also require a corresponding extension request for the one-on-one follow-up interview.
Students should be aware that extensions exceeding 14 calendar days may lead to delays in scheduling the follow-up interview, which in turn may impact the timely calculation of final results, and therefore, of the final grade not being available by the official grade submission deadline. This is particularly important for students intending to graduate or those who require timely release of results for academic progression or professional purposes.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: In-person Oral Interview
assessment_details.due_date: 8/06/2026 - 19/06/2026 Follow-up interview conducted in-person/live during Exam period. Students will sign up for an interview timeslot. This component needs to be completed by 5pm Friday, EXAM WEEK 2.
assessment_details.task_description: This In-person Oral Interview is a mandatory, identity verified assessment that functions as an academic integrity hurdle. Students who do not pass this interview will fail the course, regardless of their performance in other assessments. FORMAT & TIMING The interview will be conducted in person during Exam Weeks 1 and 2. Each interview will take approximately ten minutes. Students will sign up for an interview time with the course coordinator or a tutor and will be required to present valid student identification. Interviews will be recorded in accordance with university policy on the recording of oral assessments. PURPOSE The interview provides an opportunity for students to reflect on their learning experience in the course and to demonstrate conceptual understanding of research methods in Applied Linguistics. Students will be asked to discuss their Research Proposal, including the rationale for their research question, key methodological decisions, ethical considerations, and anticipated challenges. The interview also serves to confirm the authenticity and authorship of submitted assessment work. STATEMENT ON AI/MT This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted during the interview. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools and/or Machine Translation (MT) technologies to support their preparation, such use must be appropriately acknowledged or referenced in line with UQ’s academic integrity expectations . A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Follow-up interview conducted in-person/live during Exam period. Students will sign up for an interview timeslot. This component needs to be completed by 5pm Friday, Exam Week 2.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
While a maximum extension of 28 calendar days may be approved for the written submission, please note that an extension for the written component will also require a corresponding extension request for the one-on-one follow-up interview. The interview extension will be limited to a maximum of 7 calendar days.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
